name: Talking About the Future
summary: Italian often uses the present for near plans, plus a simple future tense.
explanation:
For plans that are close or certain, Italians frequently just use the present tense with a time word: domani vado a Milano (tomorrow I'm going to Milan). This is the easiest way to talk about the future.

There is also a dedicated future tense (il futuro semplice). Take the infinitive, drop the final -e, and add the endings -ò, -ai, -à, -emo, -ete, -anno. So parlare gives parlerò (I will speak). Note that -are verbs change the a to e in the stem.

Use the future tense for less certain or more distant plans, predictions, and promises.
pattern: present + time word, OR infinitive stem + ò/ai/à/emo/ete/anno

examples.en: Tonight we're going to the cinema.
One day we will visit Sicily.

commonMistakes: Always reaching for the future tense. For near, certain plans the present is more natural.
Forgetting that -are verbs change a to e in the future stem (parlare to parlerò).
Dropping the accent on the io and lui/lei forms (parlerò, parlerà).
Treating the future endings as different per verb group; they are the same for all.
Missing irregular future stems like essere to sarò, avere to avrò, fare to farò.
tip: Start by mastering present + a time word. Layer the true future tense in once that feels natural.
